title: Can a spin-half particle ever give more than two spots in a Stern-Gerlach experiment? -- the subtle physics of effective Hamiltonians
authors: Noah Linden, Sandu Popescu, Anthony J. Short
abstract: We show that a spin-1/2 particle can behave as if it were spin-$s$, and generate $2s+1$ spots in a Stern Gerlach measurement (albeit with a smaller gyromagnetic ratio). This arises from some subtle properties of effective Hamiltonians and Hamiltonians with constraints. Examples of implications of the effect in condensed matter are discussed. We also give some simple non-perturbative bounds for a system subjected to strong constraints.
summary: This theoretical paper demonstrates that strong coupling between a spin-half particle and another spin can induce an effective spin-$s$ behavior. This allows the particle to yield $2s+1$ distinct outcomes in a Stern-Gerlach measurement, contrary to expectations based on its intrinsic spin. The analysis relies on projecting the system dynamics onto a low-energy subspace defined by the total spin quantum number.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether a spin-half particle, when strongly coupled to another spin, can exhibit measurement outcomes in a Stern-Gerlach experiment corresponding to a higher effective spin value.
main_result: A spin-half particle can effectively behave as if it has a spin-$s$ character, leading to $2s+1$ distinct spots in the Stern-Gerlach measurement, despite its fundamental spin being $1/2$.
method: The analysis uses the theory of effective Hamiltonians to approximate the dynamics generated by the full Hamiltonian, particularly in the limit of large energy gaps ($E_0$).
model_or_system: The system involves a spin-half particle coupled to an arbitrary spin ($S_2$) via an interaction Hamiltonian ($H_0$). The measurement is modeled by coupling the system to a spatially varying magnetic field ($V_{	ext{SG}}$).
key_observables: The number of distinct spots observed in the Stern-Gerlach experiment ($2s+1$), the effective gyromagnetic ratio ($\gamma_{	ext{eff}}^1$), and the trace distance ($\mu(t)$) between state evolutions.
important_parameters: The coupling strength ($ eta$), the energy scale ($E_0$), and the spatial confinement width ($z_0$) are crucial parameters governing the validity of the approximations.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximating the evolution using effective Hamiltonians, assuming the coupling is strong enough to keep spins parallel, and that the measurement process can be bounded by a finite region.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares standard SG measurements with those involving external spin particles. Figure 2 illustrates a hypothetical three-layer material where strong inter-layer interactions constrain spins, effectively modeling a larger spin system.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the problem using the full Hamiltonian, then derives the effective Hamiltonian by projecting onto the total spin subspace ($\Pi_s$). It rigorously proves that the effective operators act like standard spin-$s$ operators, leading to the prediction of multiple spots. Finally, it analyzes the measurement fidelity using state distance metrics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to AMO physics as it explores how strong, engineered interactions (like those in coupled quantum systems or artificial lattices) can induce emergent, higher-dimensional quantum behavior from fundamentally lower-dimensional constituents.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: a) A standard Stern-Gerlach experiment for a spin-half particle. b) The same Stern- Gerlach experiment, but the spin passing through the device interacts with an external spin-half particle which remains outside the device and constrains the spins to be parallel. Note that the outer spots in the second figure are in same positions as in the first figure. c) Similar to figure b), but the spin-half particle inside the device is interacting with a spin-one particle outside.
Figure 2: a) shows a hypothetical three-layer material composed of spin 1
